skill.name: scientific-visualization
skill.description: Create publication-ready scientific figures with matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly, including multi-panel layouts, error bars, colorblind-safe palettes, journal sizing, and PDF/EPS/TIFF export guidance.
skill.summary: Create journal-ready scientific plots with accessible palettes, publication styling, and export helpers.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical combinations, external commands, network URLs, weak crypto, and credential/key patterns. Manual review found these are markdown examples, color hex values, dictionary key usage, and documentation links, not command execution, credential access, or outbound network behavior. The confirmed risk is limited to expected local file writes by helper scripts that export figures and create matplotlib style files.

low_findings.title: Local Figure and Style File Writes
low_findings.description: The helper scripts save matplotlib figures and generated style files to local paths. This is expected for the skill, but users should review output paths before running examples on sensitive directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct file writes are present and semantically tied to figure export and style template generation. No evidence found of hidden overwrite logic, credential collection, or network exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Documentation and Color Data
low_findings.description: Most external command and weak cryptography alerts occur inside markdown code fences, matplotlib examples, color palette hex values, or ordinary dictionary key handling. These are not shell execution or cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual inspection confirms the flagged lines are examples, color constants, or calls to dictionary keys. No subprocess, eval, exec, dynamic import, or cryptographic API usage was found in these contexts.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Without Runtime Network Calls
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to external documentation and color resources. They are not used by code to make requests, so the network finding is informational rather than an execution risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in comments or reference lists and no HTTP client usage was found around them. This makes outbound network behavior very unlikely.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Writes for User-Requested Exports
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts call local save and file write operations to produce publication figures and matplotlib style templates. This is an expected capability with low risk when output paths are user-controlled and reviewed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write behavior is explicit and limited to local artifacts generated by the visualization workflow. The remaining suspicious static combinations were not confirmed by semantic review.

content.value_statement: Scientific figures often fail journal checks because sizes, fonts, colors, and export formats are inconsistent. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through accessible, journal-ready plotting workflows.
actual_capabilities: Provides publication figure guidance for matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly workflows.
Includes helper scripts for saving figures as PDF, PNG, EPS, SVG, and TIFF.
Defines journal-oriented style presets for Nature, Science, Cell, PLOS, ACS, and IEEE.
Documents colorblind-safe palettes, perceptually uniform colormaps, and grayscale checks.
Shows examples for line plots, statistical plots, heatmaps, multi-panel layouts, and journal exports.
Includes figure size checks and font embedding guidance for PDF publication workflows.
limitations: Journal requirements can change, so users must verify current author guidelines before submission.
The helper scripts assume matplotlib-compatible figures and local Python dependencies.
The skill does not validate scientific conclusions or statistical model correctness.
Interactive plotly outputs may require extra export packages such as kaleido.

use_cases.title: Prepare Manuscript Figures
use_cases.description: Convert analysis plots into journal-sized figures with readable fonts, clear labels, and accepted export formats.

use_cases.title: Standardize Lab Visualization Style
use_cases.description: Create consistent palettes, typography, and sizing across figures for papers, preprints, and supplementary materials.

use_cases.title: Improve Accessibility of Data Graphics
use_cases.description: Replace unsafe color choices with colorblind-friendly palettes and redundant encodings such as markers and line styles.

prompt_templates.title: Make a Basic Plot Publication Ready
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for improving one existing chart.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my matplotlib figure plan and suggest publication-ready sizing, labels, fonts, colors, and export settings.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Journal Export Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for a target journal.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to configure a figure for Nature single-column submission, including style settings, file formats, DPI, and final checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Panel Figure
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for combining several panels.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a four-panel scientific figure with consistent axes, panel labels, colorblind-safe encodings, and export recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Figure Set for Submission
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to audit my manuscript figure set for journal compliance, accessibility, statistical display, file format, and common publication errors.

output_examples.input: A researcher asks how to save a line plot for a Nature manuscript.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends Nature-style sizing, vector-first export, 600 to 1000 DPI settings where appropriate, embedded fonts, and a final size check.

output_examples.input: A user has a red-green heatmap for expression changes.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests a colorblind-safe diverging colormap, a labeled colorbar, grayscale testing, and a caption note defining the centered scale.

output_examples.input: A lab wants a consistent style for all paper figures.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes shared matplotlib rcParams, Okabe-Ito or Paul Tol palettes, standard column widths, and repeatable export helpers.

best_practices: Start with the target journal column width, then design every panel at final print size.
Use colorblind-safe palettes with markers, line styles, or patterns for redundant encoding.
Export plots as vector files when possible and verify raster DPI for images or microscopy panels.
anti_patterns: Do not use JPEG, low DPI screenshots, or copied notebook previews for manuscript figures.
Do not rely on red-green color differences or rainbow colormaps to carry scientific meaning.
Do not omit units, sample sizes, error definitions, or statistical annotations from figure planning.

faq.question: Which plotting libraries does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly, with the strongest support for matplotlib-based publication workflows.

faq.question: Can it export figures directly?
faq.answer: It includes Python helper scripts that save matplotlib figures to local PDF, PNG, EPS, SVG, and TIFF files.

faq.question: Does it know journal-specific requirements?
faq.answer: It includes common requirements for major publishers, but users should verify current journal guidelines before submission.

faq.question: Does it support colorblind-safe visualization?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Okabe-Ito, Wong, Paul Tol, viridis, cividis, and diverging colormap guidance.

faq.question: Can it check statistics for correctness?
faq.answer: No. It can guide display of uncertainty and significance, but it does not validate study design or statistical inference.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Manual review found low risk limited to expected local file creation for exported figures and style templates.
